Metadata.title: Thai Thanh Nam | Backend Systems for Real-Time Products
Metadata.description: Backend developer building real-time systems: multiplayer game servers, monitoring pipelines, and production APIs that hold up under real concurrency.

Hero.headline: I build backend systems that stay clear, fast, and reliable when concurrency starts to matter.
Hero.summary: Backend Developer with 3+ years building multiplayer game servers, real-time communication, and monitoring for production systems. What I care about most is runtime behavior that's still understandable once real traffic shows up.

runtime.title: Runtime first
runtime.description: Connection flow, concurrency, and service behavior get my attention early, because real-time pressure exposes weak architecture fast.

observability.title: Observability from day one
observability.description: I build in monitoring, alerts, and logs while I'm building the feature, not as a fire drill after something breaks in production.

delivery.title: Delivery under real constraints
delivery.description: I ship backend work that stays maintainable even with legacy code, tight timelines, and a live product that isn't going to wait.

experience.label: Designing and running backend systems in production.

servers.label: Real-time multiplayer game server instances built with Node.js and WebSocket.

concurrency.label: Systems built to hold thousands of concurrent players without falling over.

incidents.label: Average incident response time cut by 40% after building better internal monitoring.

stack.label: SQL Server, MySQL, Redis, MongoDB, REST APIs, and real-time messaging, used daily.

Projects.title: Case studies that show systems thinking, not just feature delivery.
Projects.intro: Four case studies: production game servers, a full-stack product I helped ship, and two systems exercises I built to sharpen specific skills.

onky.title: ONKY Game Server Systems
onky.summary: Production backend ownership across ONKY's online game products: server architecture, monitoring, and live operations.
onky.context: Real-time multiplayer products, where service behavior and runtime clarity affect live players directly, and backend stability isn't optional.
onky.build: Built and extended multiplayer game servers with Node.js and WebSocket, added custom game logic on top of a shared framework, designed APIs for internal tools and lobby flows, and shipped internal SOS monitoring and process-management utilities.
onky.systems: Real-time socket communication, master-worker monitoring architecture, Redis-backed message flow, SQL-backed event logic, and incident tooling for live game instances.
onky.impact: 20+ game server instances delivered, average incident response time down 40%, redundant SQL load reduced, and production debugging and rollouts made more predictable.

vmu.title: VMU Test Exam
vmu.summary: An online exam platform for medical students, with real-time admin visibility and backend support for controlled exam sessions.
vmu.context: Students needed a testing flow that just worked. Admins needed enough visibility to monitor sessions and run the platform without guessing.
vmu.build: Worked on the backend-heavy product flows and session support connecting student activity to admin tooling, shaping the platform into something production-ready.
vmu.systems: Node.js services, Socket.IO for real-time session visibility, MongoDB persistence, and a React front end, with operational flows for exam support.
vmu.impact: Turned the system into a production-ready learning platform, with clearer moderation visibility and more reliable session handling during live exams.

tinylink.title: TinyLink
tinylink.summary: A compact URL shortener, built as a systems exercise around performance, observability, and reliability on a lean stack.
tinylink.context: A small service still has to earn its keep: fast redirects, sane data paths, and operational behavior that doesn't surprise you at 2am.
tinylink.build: Designed the backend architecture, persistence layer, cache strategy, and deployment path around practical runtime choices, not abstract scale claims nobody was asking for.
tinylink.systems: Fastify runtime, PostgreSQL persistence, Redis caching, a Dockerized delivery path, and observability patterns sized for a lean service.
tinylink.impact: A working demonstration of backend judgment: fast redirects, reliability, and maintainability, kept compact and cost-aware on purpose.

matchingHub.title: Matching Hub
matchingHub.summary: Matchmaking-as-a-service for game servers: a multi-tenant platform handling player queuing, skill-based matching, Elo ratings, and webhook delivery.
matchingHub.context: Every game studio rebuilds the same matchmaking plumbing: queues, rating windows, timeouts, result callbacks. Matching Hub centralizes that into one multi-tenant API so game servers don't have to own it.
matchingHub.build: Designed and built the platform end to end: a NestJS API with project/environment-scoped queues, three rating modes (internal Elo, external rating, or none), HMAC-signed webhook delivery with retry and backoff, and a Next.js dashboard for API keys, live pool monitoring, and match history.
matchingHub.systems: NestJS + Prisma + PostgreSQL for the API, PostgreSQL-backed in-process schedulers for webhook retries and queue timeouts (no Redis needed), a Next.js dashboard that only talks to the API server-side, and a Docker/GHCR pipeline deploying to a VPS behind a Cloudflare Tunnel, with the dashboard on Vercel.
matchingHub.impact: End-to-end platform ownership: API design, domain modeling, real security work (hashed keys, signed webhooks), and a CI/CD pipeline for a service meant to run unattended in production.

Capabilities.title: The stack matters, but the real value is what each layer lets me ship confidently.
Capabilities.intro: I group skills by what they let me build, not by a technology checklist. It maps better to how backend work actually gets delivered.

realtime.title: Event flow, sockets, and interactive runtime behavior
realtime.description: I'm comfortable in backend environments where connection handling, message timing, and event routing shape the end-user experience directly.

backend.title: API design and server-side product logic
backend.description: Service logic, REST APIs, authentication flows, internal tools, and runtime structures that stay understandable as a product grows.

data.title: SQL, Redis, and throughput-focused backend decisions
data.description: I optimize data paths where query volume, concurrency, and latency actually matter: SQL tuning, cache usage, and cutting redundant work under load.

observability.title: Monitoring, incident response, and runtime visibility
observability.description: Hands-on experience building internal monitoring and tooling that made alerting, reporting, and incident response faster in production.

delivery.title: Enough range to ship complete outcomes
delivery.description: I can also work across the rest of the stack when a project needs it: internal tools, admin systems, and full-stack product flows.

Experience.title: A path through production backend work, full-stack delivery, and real-time systems.
Experience.intro: The order matters: internships first, then product work and freelance delivery, then live game server systems under real concurrency.

onky.title: Game Server Developer
onky.summary: Owned backend architecture and runtime stability for multiple online game products, covering server behavior, APIs, data performance, monitoring, and operational tooling.
highlights: Built 20+ real-time multiplayer game servers with Node.js and WebSocket.
Designed internal APIs and backend flows for lobby systems and operational tooling.
Built SOS monitoring infrastructure with Telegram and Slack integrations.
Optimized concurrency and reduced redundant SQL work under load.

onkyIntern.title: Game Server Developer Intern
onkyIntern.summary: Started with low-level real-time backend work and system analysis, proving backend fundamentals through implementation clarity rather than framework abstraction.
highlights: Built a 1v1 Caro game server from scratch using pure Node.js and WebSocket.
Analyzed and documented an existing production game server codebase.
Implemented a new multiplayer game server from company requirements.
Completed internship tasks covering real-time backend and database handling.

freelancer.title: Web Developer
freelancer.summary: Delivered a full-stack learning and testing platform with role-aware access control, course material flows, scoring, and progress tracking.
highlights: Designed and built a full-stack application with React, Express, Node.js, and MongoDB.
Implemented JWT-based authentication and authorization.
Delivered quiz, scoring, progress tracking, and user management features.
Built a separate admin dashboard for users, lessons, and courses.

fostech.title: Web Developer
fostech.summary: Worked on frontend product delivery and integrations, contributing to product management surfaces, reporting flows, and build performance improvements.
highlights: Built UI components and product-management screens for Proshop.
Optimized Webpack configuration to improve load and build performance.
Implemented API integration for dynamic reporting in WLIN CRM.
Supported customer-relationship workflows through React-based interfaces.

fostechIntern.title: Web Developer Intern
fostechIntern.summary: Early product engineering work focused on third-party integration, payment flow correctness, and secure transaction behavior inside an existing React application.
highlights: Integrated the Megapay payment solution into an existing React application.
Implemented authentication and transaction flows from API specifications.
Enabled secure in-app payments for end users.
Completed integration work with production-minded correctness.

Principles.title: How I try to build backend systems that survive contact with production.
Principles.intro: I care less about looking clever and more about whether the system is still reliable and easy to reason about once real traffic and incidents show up.

reliability.title: Optimize for behavior under pressure
reliability.description: Good backend work should still make sense when concurrency rises, incidents happen, or a live product exposes a weak assumption.

clarity.title: Keep runtime and code paths readable
clarity.description: I'd rather have a system that's easy to inspect and debug than an architecture that only looks impressive on paper.

concurrency.title: Design with load and timing in mind
concurrency.description: Connection handling, event flow, and race conditions aren't edge cases in real-time products. They're the product.

visibility.title: Observability is part of delivery
visibility.description: Good monitoring shortens incident response and lowers the stress of being on call. That's the point of it, not a checkbox.

Contact.title: Need backend ownership for real-time systems or production stability? Let's talk.
Contact.intro: I'm a good fit for teams building multiplayer products, event-driven backends, or systems where reliability isn't optional.
Contact.availabilityValue: Open to select freelance, consulting, and recruiter conversations with a real backend or systems angle.
Contact.educationValue: Bachelor of Engineering in Computer Science (Information Technology), Ho Chi Minh City University of Technology and Education.

Blog.title: Short essays on backend systems, runtime clarity, and production tradeoffs.
Blog.description: Where I write down the engineering decisions behind stable systems and cleaner delivery, mostly so I remember why I made them.
Blog.fallbackNotice: This article is currently available in English only.

meta.title: Technical Notes
meta.description: Notes on backend systems, runtime clarity, and delivery tradeoffs, from someone who has to live with the decisions.
